q1_conscious.text: Is the patient conscious and able to respond to you?

q_alt_unconscious.text: Patient is unconscious following trauma. Check breathing now.

q_alt_route_cardiac_arrest.text: Unconscious and not breathing normally following trauma â€” this is a resuscitation situation. The cardiac arrest protocol applies.

q2_breathing_normally.text: Is the patient breathing normally, with no obvious difficulty?

q3_severe_bleeding.text: Is there severe, heavy, or spurting bleeding from any wound?
q3_severe_bleeding.guidance_note: Direct pressure is the first-line measure. A tourniquet is only appropriate for a trained bystander managing catastrophic limb bleeding not controlled by direct pressure â€” do not instruct an untrained caller to apply one.

q4_bleeding_bystander_capable.text: Is there someone able to apply firm direct pressure to the bleeding right now?

q5_mechanism_severity.text: Does the patient have any of: inability to move a limb, suspected spinal injury, person trapped or pinned, fall from height, or was ejected from the vehicle?

pre_arrival_instructions: Stay on the line with me.
Do not move the patient's neck or back unless there is immediate danger at the scene (such as fire or oncoming traffic).
If the patient is struggling to breathe and you are trained to do so, help maintain an open airway without moving the neck.
Watch closely. If the patient stops breathing or becomes unconscious, tell me immediately â€” the instructions change.
Stay on the line with me.
Apply firm, continuous direct pressure to the wound using a clean cloth or dressing.
Do not lift the pressure to check the wound â€” keep pressing firmly without stopping.
If blood soaks through, add more material on top rather than removing what is already there.
Do not move the patient's neck or back unless there is immediate danger at the scene.
Stay on the line with me.
If you are able to safely approach the patient, apply firm direct pressure to the bleeding using any clean cloth available.
If you cannot safely reach the patient, stay on the line and tell me what you can see.
Do not move the patient's neck or back unless there is immediate danger at the scene.
Stay on the line with me.
Do not move the patient unless there is immediate danger at the scene, such as fire, oncoming traffic, or a structure at risk of collapse.
Keep the patient as still as possible and reassure them help is on the way.
Watch for any change in breathing or consciousness and tell me immediately if anything changes.
Based on what you've told me, this does not appear to be immediately life-threatening, but an ambulance is still on the way to assess the patient properly.
Keep the patient still and comfortable.
If anything changes â€” worsening pain, new bleeding, difficulty breathing, or loss of consciousness â€” call back immediately.
The patient is unconscious and not breathing after trauma â€” this is a cardiac arrest.
Start CPR immediately if you are able.
Place the patient on their back on a firm surface.
Place the heel of one hand in the centre of the chest and push hard and fast at 100-120 compressions per minute.
Continue without stopping until the ambulance crew arrives.
